8. The commutation having been sanctioned, the applicant will be informed, and will have the option of withdrawing his application within 30 days after receiving that notice.

9. If the applicant shall not withdraw his application within that period, the decision will be considered final, and the commutation of pension will take place absolutely.

10. In every case the capitalized sum will be payable in India, the sterling amount being converted at the official rate of exchange for the year in which the payment is made.

11. In the case of an officer in Europe, the capitalized sum will be payable at the expiration of two months, and in the case of an officer in India, at the

expiration of one month from the date of the commutation being sanctioned. In each case the officer's pension will be issued in full up to the date of payment, unless any delay has been caused by the officer's own fault or that of his agents.

12. In any case in which a pensioner shall accept public employment, involving the withholding of pension, a deduction will be made from the salary payable to him equal to the amount which would have been abated from the pension if it had not been commuted, and in the event of his becoming entitled to a pension in respect of such employment, the amount of such pension granted to him shall not exceed the amount which he might have received had his pension not been so commuted.

CONDITIONS OF TRANSFER TO TEMPORARY HALF-PAY.

COMMISSIONED OFFICERS.

(Sanctioned by Despatch to Government of India, No. 432, dated 22nd December 1881.)

Officers placed on temporary half-pay, at the recommendation of the Medical Board of the India Office, shall be examined by the same Board as to their fitness to return to duty every two years, and if still reported unfit at the third examination, that is, after being six years on the temporary half-pay list, they shall be transferred to the retired list on permanent half-pay. An Officer reported to be unfit at the commencement of the second period, i.e., after two years on half-pay, may, however, be permitted voluntarily to retire on permanent half-pay.

Officers unable to appear before the Medical Board at the stated times will be required to furnish a certificate, signed by two medical men, as to their inability. An Officer who is continuously unable to appear will be retired permanently after six years without further report.

Officers who, in view to being restored to the active list, may wish to appear before the Board at other times than those specified, may be permitted to do so. Officers ordered periodically to appear before the Board will receive their travelling expenses and lodging allowance on the Home scale, but those appearing at other times, at their own wish, must do so at their own expense.

WARRANT OFFICERS.

(Sanctioned by Despatch to Government of India, No. 25, dated 24th January 1884.)

A Warrant Officer transferred to temporary half-pay may be permitted to remain on half-pay till his total continuous absence from duty, on furlough and half-pay together, amounts to four years. If then still unfit to return to duty, he must be pensioned on the rate of pension for which he had qualified at the time of being placed on half-pay.

No period spent on half-pay will count as service for Indian pension, nor will a Warrant Officer be eligible for promotion while he is on half-pay. A Warrant Officer replaced on the effective list, on return to duty from half-pay, will be re-employed at once, being borne as a supernumary in his grade until absorbed by the next vacancy.

Warrant Officers will be required to appear before a Medical Board at the expiration of one year on half-pay, and again, if retained on the half-pay list, at the end of the second year; and those who, in view to being restored to the effective list, may wish to appear before the Board at other times than those specified, may be permitted to do so. Warrant Officers ordered periodically to appear before the Board will be allowed, in this country, their bona fide travelling expenses, and lodging allowance at the rates stated below, but those appearing at other times, and at their own wish, must do so at their own expense.

Rates of Lodging Allowance.

Deputy Commissary ... ... ... ... ... } 2s. 3d. per diem
Honorary Surgeon ... ... ... ... ...
Assistant Commissary ... ... ... ... ... } 2s. "
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... ... ... ... ...
Conductor ... ... ... ... ... } 1s. 4d. "
Sub Conductor ... ... ... ... ...
Medical Warrant Officers without honorary Commissions ... ... ... ... ...

ISSUE OF FURLOUGH AND RETIRED PAY.

Military Furlough and Retired Pay is made up at the India Office, to the 31st January, 30th April, 31st July, and 31st October, and is payable on the 8th of the following month.

EXTRACT FROM REGULATIONS OF THE MILITARY FUND LATELY CALLED
LORD CLIVE'S.

WIDOWS' PENSIONS.

1. Officers' widows will be required to sign a declaration to the effect that their husbands did not die possessed of real and personal property to the following amounts, and that they themselves do not, with what their husbands left and from all other sources, possess, if the widow of a colonel, £4,000; a lieut.-colonel, £3,000; a major, £2,500; a captain, £2,000; a lieutenant £1,000.

2. The rates of pension are as follows:—

RANKS. Widows' Pensions.
Per dm. Per annum.
s. d. £ s. d.
Colonel, Surgeon-General, and Deputy Surgeon-General 6 3 114 1 3
Lieut.-Colonel, Brigade-Surgeon, and Surgeon-Major after 20 years' service 5 0 91 5 0
Major, Surgeon-Major, and Chaplain of 15 years' service 3 9 68 8 9
Captain, Surgeon, Chaplain of 7 years' service, and Veterinary Surgeon of 20 years' service 2 6 45 12 6
Chaplain under 7 years' service 1 9 31 18 9
Lieutenant and Veterinary Surgeon 1 3 22 16 3
  • 3. The pensions continue only during widowhood.
  • 4. Pensions lapsing on re-marriage are not restored on a second widowhood.
  • 5. Pensioners neglecting to claim the pension for three half-years will be considered as dead, and no arrears for a longer period than two years back from the date of application for admission or re-admission, as the case may be, will be allowed either to claimants or to pensioners after admission.
  • 6. The widows of officers formerly of the Indian service, who have joined the British army, retain their claims to pension from this Fund.
  • 7. The widows of officers who have retired from the service are not eligible to the benefits of this Fund.*
  • 8. The widows of officers who at the time of their death were pensioners on Lord Clive's Fund are entitled to the benefits of the Fund, provided they were married to their husbands prior to the latter having become pensioners on such Fund.
  • 9. These pensions are payable quarterly, on the 1st January, 1st April, 1st July, and 1st October.

INDIA OFFICE, March 1883.

Note.—Applications for admission to this Fund should be addressed to
The Military Secretary, India Office, London, S.W.

INDIAN MARINE.

Appointments in the Indian Marine are made by the Secretary of State for India in Council.

The limits of age for appointment to the junior executive rank, that of Third Grade Officer, are 17 and 22 years, and no candidate will be appointed who does not possess a Board of Trade certificate as Second Mate.

Applications for appointment can be recorded at the India Office, but will not be submitted for the orders of the Secretary of State until the applicant is duly qualified.

When qualified, the Board of Trade Certificate, together with certificates of birth and character, should be transmitted to the Military Secretary, India Office.

The names of candidates will be removed from the list on their attaining the age of 22 years.

On appointment a first class passage to Bombay will be provided, and an advance of £50 will be made in aid of outfit.

Information in regard to the appointment of Assistant Engineer in the Indian Marine may be obtained from the Director General of Stores, India Office.

INDIA OFFICE,
November, 1884.

* Except in the case of the widows of General Officers compulsorily retired under the provisions of the Royal Warrants of 31st December 1877, 1st May 1878, 25th June 1881, 11th March 1882, and 1st April 1884.

FURLOUGH AND LEAVE RULES FOR HER MAJESTY'S INDIAN MARINE SERVICE.MARINE CIRCULAR OF THE GOVERNMENT OF INDIA, NO. 16, DATED SIMLA, THE
1ST NOVEMBER, 1884.Furlough.

I.—Officers after five years' service in India or under the Government of India may receive one year's furlough, and an additional year for each subsequent five years' service. Furlough not taken at the time it becomes due is not lost, and can, subject to these regulations, be taken later.

II.—An officer may not be absent from duty on any grounds for more than three years consecutively, nor on return from furlough may he receive, except under Rule VII., any further furlough until after an interval of three years.

III.—Furlough does not involve forfeiture of appointment, may be spent anywhere, and, when it has been earned by length of service, counts as service for pension. Furlough taken before it is earned by length of service reckons against service for pension till paid off.

IV.—An officer on furlough receives half the pay of his rank, or, if holding a shore appointment, half the pay of his appointment excluding command, charge, acting, personal, or house allowance, computed according to a rate of exchange to be fixed in each year by the Secretary of State. An officer promoted while on furlough will be entitled to a moiety of the increased pay due to that promotion.

V.—Officers proceeding on furlough may receive an advance equivalent to three months' furlough pay; the same advance may also be granted on return from furlough. Recovery of the advances made in England will be effected in India by six equal monthly instalments.

VI.—Furlough pay may not be drawn for more than two years consecutively.

VII.—Upon certificate of a medical board, an officer not entitled to furlough, who has served not less than one year in India, may be granted furlough. Such furlough will not count as service for pension until it has been paid off by subsequent service under the terms of Rule I. Furlough cannot be anticipated beyond two years in the aggregate nor for a second term, unless that previously anticipated has been subsequently paid off under the terms of Rule I.

VIII.—An officer who has exhausted the furlough obtainable under these rules, and who may be certified by a medical board to be still unfit for duty, will be retired on the pension or gratuity to which he may be entitled; if not entitled to either pension or gratuity, his case will be specially considered.

IX.—Advances of passage money, not exceeding £50, will be granted to officers proceeding on or returning from furlough, at the discretion of the Director of the Indian Marine or the Secretary of State. Marine officers not holding shore appointments of Rs. 1,000 per mensem will be eligible for the grant of non-regulation passages in troopships when such passages are available.

X.—Furlough, whether on private affairs or on medical certificate, is restricted to a maximum period of one year in the first instance, and to subsequent extensions not exceeding six months at a time.

General Leave.

XI.—General leave may be granted for any period not exceeding six months at a time, but cannot be renewed, except for very urgent reasons, till after two years have elapsed from its expiration. It may be spent anywhere, does not involve forfeiture of appointment and counts as service for pension. It will be granted or refused entirely at the discretion of the Director of the Indian Marine. It may be granted up to a maximum limit of 30 days as subsidiary to furlough, whether on proceeding on furlough or on return from furlough. Leave granted as subsidiary to furlough can only be spent in India.

XII.—During general leave an officer will be restricted to the pay of his grade,* and will forfeit all allowances, which will be paid to his locum tenens. If an officer exceeds the six months' leave admissible at one time under this rule, the entire period will be converted into furlough, and his allowances adjusted accordingly.

Privilege Leave.

XIII.—Subject to the exigencies of the service, Marine officers may be allowed 60 days' privilege leave during each year, and such leave may be taken in instalments.

XIV.—An officer who has privilege leave to his credit when proceeding on leave or furlough does not forfeit it, but privilege leave cannot be taken in continuation of furlough, nor can furlough be obtained in continuation of privilege leave. Privilege leave may, however, be converted into general leave with the sanction of the Director of the Indian Marine, or into furlough with the sanction of the Government of India.

XV.—Privilege leave will be granted by the Director of the Indian Marine, but it will be granted only on the understanding that no additional expense is thereby caused to the State either directly or indirectly, that the applicant's services can be spared, and that satisfactory arrangements for the performance of his duties during his absence can be made.

XVI.—Officers are at liberty to proceed wherever they may desire when on privilege leave, provided they can ensure their return before the expiration of the leave. If an officer exceeds his privilege leave, the whole period of his absence will be converted into general leave or furlough, and his allowances and statement of service for furlough adjusted accordingly.

* This rule will also hold good in the case of an officer holding a shore appointment, in which case the balance will be considered a staff allowance payable to the locum tenens, provided the latter does not thereby draw more than the pay of the appointment.

RULES FOR HER MAJESTY'S INDIAN MARINE SERVICE. lxxvii

Temporary Leave.

XVII.—Temporary leave not exceeding 10 days, and not in anticipation or in extension of any other kind of leave or furlough, may be granted by the Director and Deputy Director of the Indian Marine.

XVIII.—Temporary leave not exceeding 72 hours may be granted by commanders of vessels and heads of departments to officers under their command.

General Regulations.

XIX.—It must be distinctly understood that furlough or leave cannot be claimed as a right. Both are subject to the exigencies of the public service, and will be granted only when an officer's services can be spared. Commanders of vessels and heads of departments will not forward an application for furlough or leave unless they are able to satisfy the Director of the Indian Marine that the officer's services can be spared without any inconvenience to the public service.

XX.—No grant of leave given under the conditions of these rules will count against an officer's service for pension, and leave may be spent anywhere, i.e., within possible limits of return before it expires.

XXI.—Leave is reckoned from the day on which an officer avails himself of it, and it expires on the day he rejoins.

Furlough reckons from date of leaving India or from date of leaving his ship if at an outstation, unless specially stated by authority to commence on an earlier date; and in the event of an officer returning to India before the expiration of his furlough, it ceases on the date of his return.

The day of return, whether from leave or from furlough, is a day of duty.

XXII.—Officers on furlough will draw their pay from the India Office, but officers on general or privilege leave must arrange to draw their pay in India.

XXIII.—As a rule, leave will be granted only during the period of the south-west monsoon.

XXIV.—Officers proceeding on furlough will report their departure personally at the office of the Director or Deputy Director of the Indian Marine, except when they avail themselves of it at an outstation. An officer returning from furlough or leave will rejoin the ship to which he belongs; but if this is impracticable, and he has not received instructions (which he should apply for beforehand), he will rejoin at Bombay or Calcutta, reporting himself in person to the Director or Deputy Director. On the departure of an officer on furlough or leave, or on the return of an officer therefrom, the commander of the vessel will cause an entry to be made of the date in the ship's log book, and will forward an extract thereof by the next post to the Director.

XXV.—Officers arriving in Europe on furlough must report their arrival in writing to the Military Secretary at the India Office, and state their address; and for extension of furlough or for permission to return to duty, they must also apply to the Military Secretary.

Officers proceeding to the Colonies on furlough should apply to India for extensions of furlough and for permission to return to duty.

XXVI.—During the absence of an officer on leave or furlough, he must keep the Director of the Indian Marine acquainted with his actual address, and be prepared to return immediately to India, should his services be required. When on leave, such recall order will be issued by the Director; when on furlough in Europe, by the Secretary of State. Officers spending their furlough in Europe must keep the Secretary of State informed of their address.

XXVII.—Applications for leave or furlough must be made on the authorized form, which is obtainable from the Director or Deputy Director of the Indian Marine.

INDIAN SERVICE FAMILY PENSION REGULATIONS.

REGULATIONS FOR THE PROVISION OF PENSIONS FOR THE WIDOWS AND ORPHANS OF MILITARY, MEDICAL, AND ECCLESIASTICAL OFFICERS IN THE SERVICE OF THE GOVERNMENT OF INDIA.

1 January, 1873.

1. Pensions will be provided under these regulations for the widows and orphans of the following officers on the Indian establishment, not being entitled to the benefits of any of the late Military and Medical Funds:

  • Officers of the Staff Corps.
  • Officers of the Indian Medical Department.
  • Chaplains.

2. Every European officer who enters the service on and after the 1st January, 1873, must, as a condition of his appointment, provide pensions for his family under these regulations. A Native Commissioned Medical Officer will be required to declare his intention of joining the Fund or not within three months from the date of his taking up his appointment in India.

Note—On appointment to the Service, Medical Officers and Chaplains are admitted to the Fund "from date of arrival in India."

5. For the purposes of these regulations officers are classed as follows:—

  • Class I.—Lieutenant-Colonels in receipt of Colonel's allowances, and Surgeons-General.
  • " II.—Lieutenant-Colonels, Archdeacons, Deputy Surgeons-General, Brigade Surgeons, and Surgeons-Major of 20 years' service.
  • " III.—Majors, Senior Chaplains, and Surgeons-Major.
  • " IV.—Captains, Junior Chaplains, and Surgeons who have served the Government of India for more than six years.
  • " V.—Lieutenants and Surgeons who have served the Government of India for less than six years.

6. The pensions provided under these regulations are payable monthly in India, and quarterly in arrear in England, and to date of death.

7. The amounts of the pensions provided are as follows, in addition to any pensions or compassionate allowances granted under Royal Warrant.

(N.B.—The one now in force is dated 1st April, 1884.)

TABLE I.—Pension to a Widow during Widowhood, according to the Class of her Husband at the time of his death.

CLASS OF HUSBAND AT DEATH.
I. II. III. IV. V.
Amount of pension to widow per ann.: £ £ £ £ £
In Europe ..... 160 130 100 70 40
Rs. Rs. Rs. Rs. Rs.
In India ..... 1,669 1,356 1,043 730 417

TABLE II.—Pensions to the Orphan Children of all Classes alike.

From birth till age of 6 years. From age of 6 till age of 12 years. From age of 12 till age of 21 years. To females only from age of 21 years for life, or till marriage.
Amount of pension to each orphan: £ £ £ £
In Europe ..... 10 20 30 45
Rs. Rs. Rs. Rs.
In India ..... 104 208 313 469

8. The pension of a minor orphan will be paid to his or her mother, or other duly appointed or recognized guardian.

NOTE.—Annuitants in India must draw their pensions in Indian currency.

Contributions.

9. The contributions for pensions under these regulations, and the pensions of widows, will be regulated by the class of the contributing officer. An officer cannot secure a higher pension for his widow by contributing as if he belonged to a higher class.

* As an exception to this rule, an officer who has completed five years' service as a substantive Lieutenant-Colonel has the option of subscribing in Class I. (A. C. 34, of Jan. 1881.)

10. Contributions will be deducted by paymasters or other disbursing officers from the pay abstracts of officers contributing.

10A. The circumstance that an Officer is not in receipt of pay does not relieve him from the necessity of paying his contributions.

11. Donations may be paid in one sum, or in 12 equal monthly instalments. If a donation be paid in instalments, interest on the unpaid portion will be levied at the rate of 4\frac{1}{2} per cent. per annum. The balance of any contribution due at an officer's death will be recovered rateably, without interest, from the pensions of his widow and orphans, in 12 monthly instalments in India, or four quarterly instalments in England.

11A. Contributions which become due from subscribers when in Europe must be paid in England in sterling. If a subscriber, previous to quitting India, has commenced the payment of any donation by instalments, he may either complete the payment of the donation in India before leaving, or pay the remaining instalments in England at English rates; but an Officer on half-pay who continues to subscribe at Indian rates has the option of paying in India.

12. The following are the rates of donations and monthly contributions prescribed for the present:—

TABLE III.—Donations.

Example.—A Captain, aged 37, marries a lady aged 22: the difference of age is 15, and the marriage donation is Rs. 746.

A Colonel, aged 54, marries a lady aged 29: the difference of age is 25, and the marriage donation is Rs. 5976.

Arrears.

13. Interest at the rate of 4½ per cent. per annum will be charged on arrears. Donations will become due from the date on which the liability is incurred, viz., on marriage, on promotion, or on the birth of a child, but interest will not be charged on sums paid within one month from the date on which they become due.

Reports of Marriages, Births, and Deaths.

14. An officer who is married at the date of his admission into the service must report the fact of his marriage at the time, and the names and dates of birth of his children, if any have been born to him. An officer in the service must report his marriage, or the birth of a child born in wedlock, or the death of a wife or child, within one month of the occurrence, to the Examiner of Fund Accounts in the Presidency, or the Director of Funds, India Office, when an officer is in Europe. Forms for this purpose are given in the Appendix.

15. The birth of every child born alive must be reported; but if a child die before it is thirty days old, no payment in respect of it will be required.

15A. If the report of a marriage, or of the birth of a child, be not made within one month of the occurrence, the donation payable in each case will be increased 2½ per cent., and interest thereon charged from the date of marriage or birth of the child. After six months' delay, donations will be increased 5 per cent., and interest charged in the usual manner. (G.O.G.G. No. 381 of 1877.)

15B. In the event of the report of a marriage or the birth of a child not being made by a subscriber during his lifetime, in accordance with the rules, the widow or child will not be entitled to any benefits. (Army Circulars, India, 1880, Clause II.)

Posthumous Child.

16. A pension will be granted to a posthumous child on payment of the donation prescribed in Table V., which may be recovered by 12 monthly instalments in India, or four quarterly instalments in England. The report of the birth of a posthumous child must be made in Form D., the last sentence being omitted.

Commutation of Contributions.

17. An officer who desires to complete his payments during the term of his service in India, so as to be exempt from contributions after his retirement, will be permitted to do so at rates to be determined by the tables appended to General Hannington's report, dated London, 7th June, 1870, or by any other tables that may be hereafter substituted by the Secretary of State for India in Council.

18. An officer may at any time commute his future payments into a single payment, the amount of such payment being regulated by the same tables.

Duration of Pensions.

19. If a widow remarry, her pension will cease during coverture, but if she again become a widow she will be entitled to revert to her pension as widow of her first husband, or, if her last husband have been a contributor, to receive a pension at her option as the widow of her last husband. A widow can only receive one pension.

INDIAN SERVICE FAMILY PENSION REGULATIONS. lxxxii

20. The pension of a male orphan is payable until he attains the age of 21 years.
21. The mother or guardian of a male orphan who has completed the age of 16 may, at any time thereafter (on application in Forms F to H. annexed to these regulations), have the residue of his pension commuted into a single payment, for the due application of which the mother or guardian will be held responsible. The following table gives the commuted values of such pensions:—

TABLE VIII.—Showing the present Value of the Pension of a Male Orphan, according to his Age.

Years. 16 17 18 19 20
Months. £ £ £ £ £
0 129 106 81 56 28
3 123 100 75 49 21
6 117 94 68 42 14
9 111 87 62 35 7

22. The pension of a female orphan continues until her marriage, and then ceases absolutely. It does not revive on widowhood.

Good Conduct.

23. The pensions provided under these regulations shall, in all cases, be held during good behaviour only, and will be withheld if any pensioner is found to be leading an immoral life, or on proof of any gross misconduct. The order of the Secretary of State for India in Council in any such case shall be final and conclusive.

Alienation of Pension.

24. A pension paid under these regulations may not be transferred or assigned, or in any way alienated, from the pensioner to whom or for whose benefit it is payable.

Passage Money.

25. An officer may, by a special contribution, provide passage money from India for his widow and orphans. The amount so secured will belong to the officer's estate, but will be primarily applicable to the provision of passage money and outfit for his widow and children. The amount secured must not exceed 1,500 rupees, and a health certificate must in every case be furnished by the subscriber. The value will be determined by the Tables in Major-General Hannington's report.

25A. The special contribution can be paid by monthly instalments not exceeding 24, the instalments in arrear bearing interest at 4\frac{1}{2} per cent. per annum, and in the event of a subscriber dying before completion of the payment, the balance due at his death will be deducted from the sum insured.

25B. Native commissioned medical officers are required to pay, as in the case of their ordinary contributions, 5 per cent. over and above the ordinary rates of special contribution (Army Circulars India, 1884, Clause 33).

Transfer to Her Majesty's Corps, and Officers who resign.

26. Officers who may be transferred from the Indian Staff Corps to Her Majesty's Line regiments, and officers who resign the Service, will be entitled to receive back such portion of their contributions as may be in excess of the risk borne during their membership, but they cannot continue subscribers.

Half-Pay.

27. Married officers placed on half-pay may receive back such portion of their contributions as is in excess of the risk borne during the term of their membership, or they may continue to subscribe at Indian rates (which can be paid in India) for full benefits, or at lower rates for reduced benefits. In the event of their return to full pay, they shall, in addition to the subscription of their rank, be required to pay such donation as the circumstances of the case may demand. Unmarried officers may continue to subscribe at the Europe rate of subscription, and will not be entitled to any refund in the event of their withdrawal.

Retired Officers.

28. Married officers who retire from the service on a pension may continue to subscribe at the Europe rate of subscription, or they may withdraw, receiving back such portion of their contributions as is in excess of the risk borne during the term of their membership; but the usual subscription will be deducted from the pension of all such officers until they shall have intimated in writing their intention of withdrawing. Unmarried officers may continue their subscription on the same terms as married officers, but will not be entitled on retirement to any refund in the event of their withdrawal.

Dismissed Officers.

29. An officer who is dismissed the service by sentence of a court martial, or by other competent authority, will cease, ipso facto, to be a contributor under these regulations, and will have no claim to any refund. His family will not be entitled to any pensions under these regulations.

Certificates.

30. Declarations as to continued widowhood and the survival of children must be furnished to the Accountant-General, India Office, by whom pensions in Europe are paid under the authority of the Director of Funds.

Note A.—The contributions and donations to be paid by Native Members of the Covenanted Medical Service are fixed for the present at an increase of 5 per cent. on the rates paid by Europeans.

APPENDIX.

Note.—In England all reports and applications for pensions must be made to the Director of Funds, India Office, London, S.W.

For the present, the rate of interest is fixed at 4\frac{1}{2} per cent. per annum, and the rate of exchange with England at 1s. 8d. the rupee.

FORM (B.) of a Letter from an Officer reporting his having joined the service of the Government of India married. (See Regulation 14.)

To the Examiner of Fund Accounts for the Presidency of

Sir,—With reference to the Family Pension Regulations published with the Notification by the Government of India, in the Military Department, No. 1315, dated 28th December, 1872, I have the honour to report that I was admitted to the service of the Government of India, married, on the 18

We were married on

A certified copy of the register of our marriage is herewith submitted.

The names and birthdays of my children are as follows:—

Sir,—With reference to the Family Pension Regulations published with the Notification by the Government of India, in the Military Department, No. 1315, dated 28th December 1872, I have the honour to report my marriage to Miss

FORM (D.) of a Letter reporting the Birth of a Child. (See Regulations 14 and 15.)

To the Examiner of Fund Accounts for the Presidency of

Sir,—I have the honour to report the birth to me of a male (female) child on the may and I request that the name be entered in the pension register kept under the regulations published with Notification by the Government of India, in the Military Department, No. 1315, dated 28th December 1872, and that the deduction thereby prescribed may be made from my pay.

FORM (E.)—Report of Death. (See Regulation 14.)

To the Examiner of Fund Accounts for the Presidency of

Sir,—I have the honour to report the decease on the of my son (daughter) born on the (or of my wife) A death certificate is herewith transmitted.

FORM (F.) of application for the commuted value of the pension of a Male Orphan over 16 years of age. (See Regulation 21.)

Sir,—I, A. B., being the mother (or guardian) of C. D., son of E. F., late a in the service of the Government of India, having now an opportunity of placing my son (or ward) in a suitable position, request that the commuted value of the remaining portion of his pension may be granted to me under my guarantee for its proper application to his use.

The age of my son (or ward) is now years months, and I submit herewith a certificate of his health.

FORM (G.).—Medical Certificate to be furnished by Mother or Guardian applying for commutation of a Male Orphan's Annuity. (See Regulation 21.)

We do hereby certify that we have carefully and personally examined into the state of the health of C. D., son of the late E. F., and that we consider him free from any disease likely to prevent his attaining the age of 21 years.

(Sd.) G. H. } M.D.
" I. K. }

FORM (H.).—Mother or Guardian's Receipt for Commuted Value of a Male Orphan's Annuity. (See Regulation 21.)

I, A. B., mother (or guardian) of C. D., son of the late E. F., in consideration of the payment to me of the sum of , being the commuted value of the pension receivable by the said C. D., from till he attains the age of 21 years, do hereby bind myself to account, if called on by the Secretary of State for India, for the due application of the said sum for the use and benefit of the said C. D.

WARRANT OF PRECEDENCE IN INDIA.

VICTORIA, by the grace of God, of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland, Queen Defender of the Faith, Empress of India,—

To all to whom these presents shall come greeting

WHEREAS it hath been represented unto Us that it is advisable that the rank and precedence of persons holding appointments in the East Indies, as regulated by Our Royal Warrant, dated the 6th day of May, 1871, should be altered in manner hereinafter mentioned, We do therefore hereby declare that it is Our Will and Pleasure, notwithstanding anything in Our said Warrant contained, that the following Table be henceforth observed with respect to the rank and precedence of the persons hereinafter named, viz. :—

  1. 1. Governor-General and Viceroy of India.
  2. 2. Governors of Madras and Bombay.
  3. 3. President of the Council of the Governor-General.
  4. 4. Lieutenant-Governor of Bengal, the North-Western Provinces, or the Punjab, when in his own territories.
  5. 5. Commander-in-Chief in India.
  6. 6. Lieutenant-Governors of Bengal, the North-Western Provinces, and the Punjab.
  7. 7. Chief Justice of Bengal.
  8. 8. Bishop of Calcutta, Metropolitan of India.
  9. 9. Ordinary Members of the Council of the Governor-General.
  10. 10. Commanders-in-Chief in Madras and Bombay.
  11. 11. Commander-in-Chief of Her Majesty's Naval Forces in India, unless senior in relative rank to the Commander-in-Chief of Madras and Bombay, in which case he will take the precedence.
  12. 12. Chief Justices of Madras, Bombay, and the North-Western Provinces.
  13. 13. Bishops of Madras and Bombay.
  14. 14. Ordinary Members of Council in Madras and Bombay.
  15. 15. Chief Commissioners and Resident at Hyderabad, and Agents to the Governor-General in Rajputana, Central India, and Baroda.
  16. 16. Puisne Judges of the High Courts of Calcutta, Madras, Bombay, and the North-Western Provinces.
  17. 17. Military Officers above the rank of Major General.
  18. 18. Additional Members of the Council of the Governor-General, when assembled to make Laws, &c.
  19. 19. Secretaries to the Government of India.
  20. 20. Commissioner in Sind.
  21. 21. Judges of the Chief Court, Punjab.
  22. 22. Additional Members of the Councils of the Governors of Madras and Bombay, when assembled to make Laws, &c.
  23. 23. Chief Secretaries to the Governments of Madras and Bombay.
  24. 24. Members of the Legislative Council of the Lieutenant-Governor of Bengal.
  25. 25. Vice-Chancellors of Indian Universities.

FIRST CLASS.

  1. 26. Civilians of 31 years' standing and Major-Generals.
  2. 27. Advocate-General, Calcutta.
  3. 28. Advocates-General, Madras and Bombay.
  4. 29. Members of the Boards of Revenue, Madras, Bengal, and the North-Western Provinces, and Commissioners of Revenue and Customs, Bombay.
  5. 30. Financial Commissioner, Punjab.
  6. 31. Judicial Commissioners, and the Recorder of Rangoon.
  7. 32. Comptroller-General of Accounts in India.
  8. 33. Commissioners of Divisions within their own Divisions, and Residents, Political Agents, and Superintendents on pay of Rs. 2,000 per mensem or more (not being Collectors or Deputy Commissioners of British Districts) within their own charges.
  9. 34. Civil and Military Secretaries to Governments, Madras and Bombay, and Civil Secretaries to Governments, Bengal, North-Western Provinces, and Punjab.
  10. 35. Surveyor-General of India, and Directors-General of the Post Office, and of Telegraphs.
  11. 36. Chief Engineers, 1st Class.
  12. 37. Archdeacons of Calcutta, Madras and Bombay.
  13. 38. Brigadiers-General.

SECOND CLASS.

  1. 39. Civilians of 23 years' standing and Colonels.
  2. 40. Commissioners of Divisions and Commissioner of Police, Calcutta.
  3. 41. Private Secretary to the Viceroy.
  4. 42. Residents, Political Agents, and Superintendents on pay of Rs. 2,000 per mensem or more (not being Collectors or Deputy Commissioners of British Districts).
  5. 43. Superintendent, Great Trigonometrical Survey.
  6. 44. Commissioner of Inland Customs.
  7. 45. Sanitary Commissioner with the Government of India.
  8. 46. Superintendent of the Geological Survey.
  9. 47. Inspector-General of Forests in India.
  10. 48. Standing Counsel to the Government of India.
  11. 49. Military Accountant-General.
  12. 50. Directors of Public Instruction under Local Governments.
  13. 51. Accountants-General for Local Governments.

52. Inspectors-General of Police under Local Governments.

53. Director of Revenue Settlement, and Superintendent of Revenue Survey, Madras; Survey and Settlement Commissioners, Bombay; Commissioner of Settlements, Punjab.

54. Remembrancers of Legal Affairs and Government Advocates in the North-Western Provinces, the Punjab, and British Burma.

55. Consulting Engineers to the Government of India for Guaranteed Railways, Calcutta and Lahore, and Chief Engineers, Second and Third Classes, under Local Governments.

56. District and Sessions Judges, Collectors and Magistrates of Districts, Deputy Commissioners of Districts, Deputy Superintendent of Port Blair, and the Chief Officer of each Presidency Municipality, within their respective charges.

57. Officers in the First Class, Graded List of Civil Offices not reserved for Members of the Covenantal Civil Service.

THIRD CLASS.

58. Civilians of 18 years' standing and Lieutenant-Colonels.

59. Political Agents and Superintendents on pay of Rs. 1,000 per mensem, but less than Rs. 2,000 (not being Collectors or Deputy Commissioners of British Districts) within their own charges.

60. Military Secretary to the Government, Punjab, and Civil Secretaries to Local Administrations.

61. Private Secretaries to Governors.

62. Directors of Public Instruction under Local Administrations.

63. Administrators-General, Calcutta, Madras, and Bombay.

64. Inspectors-General of Jails and of Registration, Sanitary Commissioners, Inspectors and Conservators of Forests under Local Governments, and Postmasters-General.

65. Accountants-General for Local Administrations.

66. Consulting Engineer to the Government of India for Guaranteed Railways, Lucknow, and Chief and Superintending Engineers when Secretaries to Local Administrations, or to Agents to the Governor-General.

67. Inspectors-General of Police under Local Administrations.

68. Senior Chaplains.

69. Superintendent of Marine, Bombay, and Deputy Director of Indian Marine.

70. Master Attendants and Port Officers.

71. Sheriffs within their own charges.

72. Officers in the Second Class, Graded List of Civil Offices not reserved for Members of the Covenantal Civil Service.

FOURTH CLASS.

73. Civilians of 12 years' standing and Majors.

74. Political Agents and Superintendents on pay less than Rs. 1,000 per mensem, within their own charges.

75. Government Solicitors.

76. Inspectors-General of Jails and of Registration, Sanitary Commissioners, and Conservators of Forests under Local Administrations.

77. Officers in the Third Class, Graded List of Civil Offices not reserved for Members of the Covenantal Civil Service.

Officers in the above table will take precedence in order of the numbers of the entries. Those included in one number will take precedence inter se according to the date of entry into that number.

When an officer holds more than one position in the table he will be entitled to the highest position accorded to him.

Officers who are temporarily officiating in any number in the table will rank in that number below permanent incumbents.

All officers not mentioned in the above table, whose rank is regulated by comparison with rank in the army, to have the same rank with reference to civil servants as is enjoyed by Military Officers of equal grades.

All other persons who may not be mentioned in this table to take rank according to general usage, which is to be explained and determined by the Governor-General in Council in case any question shall arise.

Nothing in the foregoing Rules to disturb the existing practice relating to precedence at Native Courts, or on occasions of intercourse with Natives, and the Governor-General in Council to be empowered to make rules for such occasions in case any dispute shall arise.

All ladies to take place according to the rank herein assigned to their respective husbands, with the exception of wives of Peers, and of ladies having precedence in England independently of their husbands, and who are not in rank below the daughters of Barons, such ladies to take place according to their several ranks, with reference to such precedence in England, immediately after the wives of Members of Council at the Presidencies in India.

Given at Our Court at Balmoral, this eighteenth day of October, in the year of our Lord One thousand eight hundred and seventy-six, and in the fortieth Year of Our Reign.

By Her Majesty's Command.

(Signed) SALISBURY.

WARRANT OF PRECEDENCE IN INDIA.

SUPPLEMENTARY GRADED LIST OF CIVIL OFFICES NOT RESERVED FOR
MEMBERS OF THE COVENANTED CIVIL SERVICE, PREPARED UNDER THE
ORDERS OF THE GOVERNOR GENERAL IN COUNCIL.

FIRST CLASS—(Number 57 of the Warrant).

Deputy Comptroller General.

Deputy Secretaries to the Government of India.

Deputy Surveyors General and Superintendents
of Revenue Survey.

Directors of State Railways.

Inspector of Local Offices of Account.

SECOND CLASS—(Number 72 of the Warrant).

Chairman of the Port Trust, Calcutta.

Chairman of the Town Council, Bombay.

Commissioners of Police, Madras and Bombay.

Deputy Accountants General to Local Govern-
ments.

Deputy Commissioners of Districts and of
Settlements.

Deputy Director General of the Post Office.

Deputy Director General of Telegraphs.

Deputy Inspectors General of Police.

Deputy Superintendents of Revenue, Topographi-
cal and Trigonometrical Surveys, 1st grade.

Deputy Superintendent of Port Blair.

Directors of Traffic and Construction, Telegraph
Department.

Educational Department Officers, 1st grade.

Examiners of Accounts, Public Works Depart-
ment, 1st class and 2nd class, 1st grade.

First Judges of Presidency Courts of Small
Causes.

Geological Survey Officers, 1st grade.

Government Astronomer, Madras.

Superintendent of Royal Botanical Gardens,
Calcutta.

Superintendent of Marine Survey.

Superintendent of Inam Settlements, Mysore.

Superintendents of Revenue Survey and Assess-
ment, Bombay and Mysore.

Superintending Engineers, Public Works Depart-
ment.

Under Secretaries to the Government of India.

Commanders in the Indian Marine.

THIRD CLASS—(Number 77 of the Warrant).

Assistant Secretaries to the Government of
India.

Deputy Commissioner, Inland Customs.

Deputy Commissioner of Police, Calcutta.

Deputy Directors of Revenue Settlements, and
of Revenue Surveys, Madras.

Deputy Superintendents of Revenue, Topo-
graphical and Trigonometrical Surveys, 2nd
grade.

Educational Department Officers, 2nd grade.

Examiners of Accounts, Public Works Depart-
ment, 2nd class, 2nd grade.

Executive Engineers, Public Works Department,
1st grade.

Geological Survey Officers, 2nd grade.

Inspector-in-Chief, Cotton Frauds, Bombay.

Junior Judge of Presidency Courts of Small
Causes.

Presidency Magistrates.

Registrars to the High Courts and to the Chief
Court, Punjab.

Settlement Officer, Sind.

Superintendent of the Indian Museum.

Superintendents of Stamps and Stationery.

Superintendents, Telegraph Department, 1st and
2nd grades.

First Grade Officers of six years' seniority in the
Indian Marine, and Chief Engineers in that
service.

THE MOST EXALTED ORDER OF THE
STAR OF INDIA,†

INSTITUTED 1861. ENLARGED 1866, 1875, and 1876

SOVEREIGN OF THE ORDER.

The QUEEN.

GRAND MASTER OF THE ORDER.

(His Excellency THE EARL OF DUFFERIN, G.C.B., K.P., G.C.M.G., G.M.I.E.), the
VICEROY and GOVERNOR GENERAL of INDIA for the time being.

ORDER OF THE INDIAN EMPIRE.

INSTITUTED 1ST JANUARY, 1878.†

The QUEEN.

INSTITUTED 1st JANUARY, 1878.

SOVEREIGN OF THE ORDER,—The QUEEN.

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA.

VICEROY AND GOVERNOR-GENERAL OF INDIA, The Right Hon. The EARL OF DUFFERIN, K.P., K.C.B., G.C.M.G.

Took his seat 13 Dec., 1884.

PERSONAL STAFF OF THE GOVERNOR-GENERAL.

Private Secretary.—D. Mackenzie Wallace

Asst. Private Secretary.—D. Panioty

Military Secretary.—Capt. Lord W. L. De la Poer Beresford, V.C., 9th Lancers.

Aides-de-Camp.

Major H. Cooper, N. Lane. Regt.

Lieut. the Hon. C. Harbord, Scots Guards.

Extra Aides-de-Camp.

Lieut. Lord H. A. Russell, Gren. Guards

Native Aides-de-Camp.

Subadar Major Nutha Sing, Sirdar Bahadur, 23rd N.I.

Subadar Major Bulbhudder Naigee, 5th Ghoorkas Surgeon.

Surg. Maj. J. Anderson, C.I.E.

COUNCIL.

Extraordinary Member, Gen. Sir Donald Martin Stewart, Bart., G.C.B., C.I.E., Commander-in-Chief. Took his seat 8th April, 1881.

Ordinary Members.

James Gibbs, C.S.I., C.I.E. Took his seat 10th May, 80—on furl.

Lt.-Gen. T. F. Wilson, C.B., C.I.E., B.S.C. Took his seat 2nd May, 1881.

Courtenay Peregrine Herbert, C.I.E. Took his seat 11th May, 82

Sir Stewart C. Bayley, K.C.S.I., C.I.E. Took his seat 11th May, 82.

Theodore C. Hope, C.S.I., C.I.E. Took his seat 22nd July 82

Sir Auckland Colvin, K.C.M.G., C.I.E. Took his seat 11th October, 83

Extraordinary Members, The Governors of the other presidencies, when the Council shall assemble within their territory.

Additional Members, for the purpose of making Laws and Regulations, nominated under 24 & 25 Vict. c. 67, sec. 10.

W. W. Hunter, C.S.I., C.I.E., LL.D.

H. J. Reynolds

H. S. Thomas

G. H. P. Evans

Kristodas Pal, C.I.E., Rai Bahadur

Maharajah Luchmessur Singh, of Durbhunga

J. W. Quinton

Thomas Mitchell Gibbon, C.I.E.

Secretary to the Council.—D. Fitzpatrick.

Secretary, E. C. Buck—on furl.

Secretary, D. M. Barbour

LAW DEPARTMENT.HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE.

ESTABLISHED 1862.

Chief Justice—Hon. Sir R. Garth, Kt., Q.C.

Judges—Romesh Chunder Mitter, B.L., H. S. Cunningham, W. F. McDonell, H. C., H. T. Prinsep, Arthur Wilson, L. R. Tottenham, A. T. Maclean—on furl., C. D. Field, J. F. Norris, Q.C., J. Q. Pigot, B.A., James O'Kinealy.

LAW OFFICERS.

Advocate General—G. C. Paul, B.A., C.I.E.

Standing Counsel—A. Phillips

Solicitor—R. L. Upton

Supt. and Remembrancer—T. T. Allen—on furl.

Depty. do.—G. C. Kilby—on furl.

Chief Reporter and Reporter for Indian Law Reports—J. V. Woodman—on furl.

Reporters for Indian Law Reports—T. A. Pearson, P. O'Kinealy, H. T. Hyde

Special Govt. Pleader—Annoda Persad Banerjee

Junior Govt. Pleader—Jaggodanand Mookerjee

OFFICERS OF THE COURT.

Taxing Officer, Registrar (Original Jurisdiction), Keeper of Records, and Accountant Gen.—R. Belchambers

Asst. Registrar—W. R. Fink

Registrar, Appellate Jurisdiction—J. Crawfurd, B.A.

Deputy Registrar Do.—J. H. Belchambers

Asst. Registrar, Appellate Jurisdiction—Thomas C. Ledlie

Clerk of the Crown, C. Piffard

Chief Clerk—C. T. Davis—on furl.

Coroner—K. B. Stuart

Receiver—J. C. Macgregor

Official Trustee—F. J. Fergusson—on furl.

Official Assignee—A. B. Miller

MEMO.—The Superintendents of the Central Jails (except Allahabad) are also Superintendents of the District Jails at the same stations. The remaining District Jails are usually in charge of the Civil Surgeons of the several Districts.

"Leswarree"—"Hindoostan"—"Alma"—"Balaklava"—"Inkerman"—"Sovastopo"—"Central India"—
"Afghanistan, 1879-80.

Blue. Busby-bag, Scarlet.—Plume, Red and White

Arrived in India 15 Jan., 1879. Meerut.

Depôt at CANTERBURY.

BRITISH CAVALRY.9th (Queen's Royal) Lancers.

Royal Cypher within the Garter.
"Peninsula" — "Punniar" — "Sobraon" — "Punjab" — "Chillianwallah" — "Goojerat" — "Delhi" — "Lucknow" — "Charasiah" — "Kabul, 1879" — "Kandahar, 1880" — "Afghanistan 1878-80."

Blue. Facings Scarlet.—Plume, Black and White.

Arrived at Bombay from England 10th February 1875. Umballa.

Depôt at Canterbury.

BRITISH CAVALRY.17th (Duke of Cambridge's Own) Lancers.

Death's Head—"Or Glory."

"Alma" - "Balaklava."—"Inkerman."—"Sevastopol."—"Central India"-"South Africa, 1879."

Blue.—Facings and Plume, White.

Arrived in India October 28th 1879.—Lucknow.

Depôt at Canterbury.

Colonel in Chief.—His Royal Highness G. W. F. C. Duke of Cambridge, K.G., K.P., K.T., G.C.S.I., G.C.M.G., G.C.B.

The Paschal Lamb. The Royal Cypher within the Garter. The Sphinx.

"Pristinæ virtutis memor"—"Vel exuviae triumphant."

"Egypt"—"Vimiera"—"Corunna"—"Salamanca"—"Vittoria"—"Pyrenees"—"Nivella"—"Toulon"—"Peninsula"—"Affghanistan"—"Ghuznee"—"Khelat"—"South Africa, 1851-2-3"—"Taku Forts"—"Pekin."

Scarlet—Facings Blue.

Landed at Bombay from Malta, 1st March, 1878.—Cawnpore.

MADRAS.

GOVERNOR,

The Right Hon. M. E. GRANT DUFF, C.I.E.

XII. (Prince of Wales' Royal) Lancers.

The Plume of the Prince of Wales, the Rising Sun and the Red Dragon. "Egypt" (with the Sphinx)—
"Peninsula"—"Waterloo"—"South Africa, 1851-2-3"—"Sevastopol"—"Central India."

Uniform Blue—Facings and Plume Scarlet.

Arrived in India on the 31st January, 1877. Bangalore.—Arrived on the 13th March, 1882
from Secunderabad.

XIV. (King's) Hussars.

The Royal Crest within the Garter. The Prussian Eagle. "Douro"—"Talavera"—"Fuente d'Onor"—
"Salamanca"—"Vitoria"—"Orthes"—"Peninsula"—"Punjab"—"Chillianwallah"—"Goojerat"
— "Persia"—"Central India."

Uniform Blue—Busby-Bag, yellow; Plume, white.

Arrived in India on the 7th February, 1876. Secunderabad.—Arrived on the 23rd January
and 21st February, 1882, from South Africa.

"Queen's Own" Sappers and Miners.

Seringapatam"—"Java"—"Egypt" (with the Sphinx)—"Assaye"—"Mahidpoor"—"Nagpore"—"Ava"—"Lucknow"—"Central India"—"Afghanistan, 1878-80"—"Egypt, 1882"—"Tel-el-Kebir."

A Company bears on its appointments "Taku Forts," and "Pekin."

A, B, and F Companies bear on their appointments, "A Dragon," wearing an Imperial Crown, with the word "China."

A, C, and E Companies, "Pegu."

B Company bears on its appointments "Pegu" and "Persia."

C Company bears on its appointments "Meeanee" and "Hyderabad, 1843."

G, H, and K Companies "Abyssinia."

K Company "Taku Forts."

The Lion of England. The Royal Cypher within the Garter.

BOMBAY RETIRED OFFICERS.

(The names of General Officers of the Local Service retired under the Royal Warrant of 31st December, 1877, and of those of the Staff Corps and Royal (late Indian) Artillery and Engineers retired under the Royal Warrants of 13th August, 1877, 1st May, 1878, and 25th June, 1881, will be found at pages 200, 202, 204 and 205.)

JUST PUBLISHED.

A DICTIONARY
OF
URDŪ, CLASSICAL HINDĪ
AND ENGLISH,

BY

JOHN T. PLATTS, M.A.,

Persian Teacher at the University of Oxford. Late Inspector of
Schools, Central Provinces, India.

Pages 1,260 SUPER-ROYAL OCTAVO, CLOTH.

PRICE, £3 3s. 0d.

LONDON: W. H. ALLEN & CO., 13, WATERLOO PLACE.
1884.

INDEX.—ACTIVE SERVICE.

NOTE.—Native names will be found under the first portion of the name, omitting the following words when used as titles:—Babu, Bakhshi, Haji, Jemadar, Kazi, Khan Bahadur, Khan Sahib, Khwajah, Maharaiah, Maulvi, Mir, Mirza, Nawab, Pundit, Rai, Rai Bahadur, Rajah, Rao, Rao Banadur, Rao Saheb, Seth, Shahzadah Shaikh, Sirdar, Subadar, Syud, and Wazirzadah.
